author: @martinwilliams9866
text: I had the idea of contra-rotating blades within a duct, one set of blades embedded with coils with the part off the duct surrounding it with magnets & the other blade with magnets & the part of the duct surrounding it with conductors, giving three areas where conductors are cutting through magnetic lines of force.

author: @wetgen
text: Robert, please do the world a huge favor and adapt this model for use in bi-directional tidal applications - where the tides reverse direction at coastal river outflows for instance.  To do this would be simple: add a one-way freewheel bearing in each rotor hub (each bearing will be reversed).  That will allow each rotor to contra-rotate from the other.  You will also have to reverse the blades' orientation (mirror image).  With this configuration, the tidal turbine can be rigidly fixed to the sea floor and, regardless of the reversing tidal flows, the connecting drive shaft will always rotate the generator in one direction!

author: @DonnaF-yi4jx
text: Betz theorem tells us you get %59.3 max and from your first %59.3 max. So in real life you are better off have two separate turbines. So why waste your time ?

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text: Betz Theorem alone won't work, you also need to take into account the Sail Interaction effect, where one sail directs wind into the other.

replies.author: @DonnaF-yi4jx
replies.text: @TimeSurfer206 The point is it goes downhill from there.

author: @only_justin_case
text: I'm sure I'm not the first person to mention this, but regarding the calculation of rotational speed based on the strobe effect that gives the rotor an appearance of being static, it will appear static at rotational speeds of 500rpm and 1000rpm as well as the 1500rpm you mentioned. Sorry to be pedantic and thanks so much for these really interesting videos.

author: @nedstar7378
text: The reason why the blades broken is that the surface is not smooth. Because of printing, the raw surface has small cuts of lines. If you make the surface smooth after printing, it will not broken so easily. To make it quick smooth you can rubbing the surface with thinner.

author: @edwardcunningham6315
text:
Great video once again 👍.
The serpentine copper coil is a definite improvement on the generator. I'm working on one with 18 horseshoe magnets. I was thinking about using magnetic needle bearings to further reduce any drag but the thought of interference from the horseshoe magnets comes into question. I am also thinking of incorporating a long cowling for wind tracking 😊.
❤️🙏

author: @friedrich_k
text: if the front rotor is correctly designed, tte rear potor will produce about 4 % of the power (according to Betz). This "dual rotor" design was tested and failed 50 or 60 years ago.
